ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai green curry with seafood. FOODON_03311434 (curry food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Curry dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Thai green curry with mixed seafood.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Thai green curry seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry seafood is approximately 350 g, typically comprising a generous portion of mixed seafood in coconut-based green curry sauce, served alongside steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Green Curry with Seafood — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (prawns, squid, fish, coconut milk, green curry paste) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai green curry seafood exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for prawns (FDC 175180), squid (FDC 175168), fish fillet (FDC 175159), coconut milk (FDC 169411), and green curry paste, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Thai curry dishes. Approximate ingredient proportions: seafood mix 40%, coconut milk 30%, curry paste and aromatics 15%, water/stock 15%. Nutrient values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coconut milk-based Thai curry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for coconut milk curry and mixed seafood dishes; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai green curry seafood. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous coconut milk curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are coconut milk sugars and trace starch from curry paste. High fat and protein content from seafood and coconut milk significantly attenuates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (38 × 13.3 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 5.1, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and curry paste
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1470 mg per standard 350 g serving), this dish contributes a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced fish sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load due to high protein, fat, and minimal starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 38 and a GL of approximately 5 per serving, this dish has a low glycemic impact; however, the high saturated fat content from coconut milk warrants attention for individuals managing insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk concurrently.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides approximately 5.8 g saturated fat per 100 g primarily from coconut milk, which may raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and consider requesting reduced coconut milk preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Green Curry Seafood (แกงเขียวหวานอาหารทะเล, Kaeng Khiao Wan Ahaan Thale) is a classic Thai coconut milk curry featuring a medley of mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, and white fish — simmered in a fragrant green curry sauce made from fresh green chillies, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai basil, and fish sauce blended into a rich coconut milk base. The dish is characterised by its vibrant green colour, creamy yet light texture, and a complex flavour profile balancing heat, sweetness from coconut milk, and umami from fish sauce and seafood. It is a staple of Thai cuisine and widely served in Thai restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy food at approximately 112 kcal per 100 g, providing a good source of lean protein (8.5 g/100 g) from the seafood, with fat predominantly from coconut milk (7.2 g/100 g, of which 5.8 g is saturated). Carbohydrate content is low (3.8 g/100 g), giving the dish a low glycemic index of approximately 38 and a low glycemic load per serving. Sodium is elevated at approximately 420 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and curry paste, making it a moderate concern for individuals with hypertension. The seafood component provides vitamin B12, phosphorus, zinc, and omega-3 fatty acids. Clinically, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and GL, but the saturated fat and sodium content require attention for those managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. The restaurant variant 'R10 Green Curry Seafood Caribbean' from Saap Saap Thai (Funan, Singapore) incorporates a Caribbean-inspired twist while retaining the core Thai green curry profile.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai green curry seafood was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data using plausible ingredient proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for coconut milk curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-characterised and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients using USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA data; no exact dish-level entry was found in approved primary sources. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data for this dish type. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data for this dish type. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source data for this dish type. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as coconut milk and seafood do not contribute industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate of 38 based on coconut milk curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this specific dish. 7. The restaurant menu name 'R10 Green Curry Seafood Caribbean' reflects a proprietary menu designation from Saap Saap Thai (Funan, Singapore) with a Caribbean-inspired variation; the canonical food name is normalised to 'Green Curry Seafood'. 8. Halal status is listed as false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; the dish may be halal-certified at specific outlets. 9. The cuisine_tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the input record are not applicable to this dish and have been discarded as field survey platform tagging artefacts. 10. Serving size of 350 g is estimated for a standard restaurant portion of curry dish without rice.
